annotator S.report_text:
To compromise the victims, the attackers used extremely effective social engineering techniques which involved sending malicious PDF documents to their targets.
An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution.
These malicious PDF files were rigged with exploits targeting Adobe Reader versions 9, 10 and 11, bypassing its sandbox.
Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities in client applications to execute code.
This downloader is unique per system and contains a customized backdoor written in Assembler.
Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.
When loaded at system boot adversaries may configure system settings to automatically execute a program during system boot or logon to maintain persistence or gain higher-level privileges on compromised systems.
the downloader uses a set of mathematical calculations to determine the computer's unique fingerprint.
Adversaries may do this to gather information.
encrypt its communications later.
Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol.
the malware will use Twitter (unbeknownst to the user) and start looking for specific tweets from pre-made accounts.
the tweets maintain specific tags labeling encrypted URLs for the backdoors.
Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.
These URLs provide access to the C2s, which then provide potential commands and encrypted transfers of additional backdoors onto the system via GIF files.
Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.
the malware can use Google Search to find the encrypted strings to the next C2.
Adversaries may register for web services.
Using common services, such as those offered by Google or Twitter, makes it easier for adversaries to hide in expected noise.
it receives encrypted backdoors that are obfuscated within GIF files and disguised as pictures that appear on a victim's machine.
Adversaries may use steganography techniques in order to prevent the detection of hidden information.
Steganographic techniques can be used to hide data in digital media such as images, audio tracks, video clips, or text files.
if Twitter isn't working or the accounts are down, the malware can use Google Search to find the encrypted strings to the next C2.
Adversaries may use fallback or alternate communication channels if the primary channel is compromised or inaccessible in order to maintain reliable command and control and to avoid data transfer thresholds.
through functions such as copy file, move file, remove file, make directory, kill process and of course, download and execute new malware and lateral movement tools.
Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.
Once they are downloaded to the machine, adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.
The final stage backdoor connects to two servers, one in Panama and one in Turkey to receive the instructions from the attackers.
Commands to the remote system, and often the results of those commands, will be embedded within the protocol traffic between the client and server.
